learning_outcomes.description: possess a comprehensive understanding and extensive knowledge of the Scientist-Practitioner Model and articulate the importance of evidence-based practice in psychology
apply an evidence-based practice approach, according to the Scientist-Practitioner Model, to issues across diverse populations in applied psychology
identify key assessment tools and their psychometric properties to apply them correctly across a range of applied disciplines and contexts
use appropriate oral and written communication skills as part of evidence-based practice
apply ethical frameworks and standards to key issues in applied psychology and reflect on how your values and experience influence evidence-based practice

demonstrate appropriate interpersonal communication and interview skills in situations appropriate to psychological practice and research. This includes active listening, clarifying and reflecting, effective questioning, summarising and paraphrasing, developing rapport, appropriate cultural responsiveness and empathic responding.

identify culturally safe and relational approaches in providing healthcare services for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, and how this intersects with professional practice, research, and community engagement in psychology

assessments.assessment_title: In-Tutorial, Secure Online Quiz (Multiple Choice and Short Answer)
assessments.due_date:
18/08/2026 - 21/08/2026

We will conduct the online quiz during your usual tutorials. Please bring a device (tablet, laptop, or handheld computer) capable of accessing the internet to participate and answer the series of multiple-choice and short answer questions.
assessments.task_description:
This quiz will assess students on the topic Cultural safety in working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations (Week 2 lecture content and associated materials) as well as the HMBS Faculty Cultural Safety in Practice, module 3

Format: Multiple-choice and short answer

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Must attend tutorials to access and complete the quiz.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: If you are unable to attempt the online quiz at the time specified and wish to request a deferred quiz, please complete the online application for deferral of an assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Interpersonal Communication and Interview Skills - Video Role-play
assessments.due_date:
19/10/2026 5:00 pm

Please use ECHO360 or a comparable platform (e.g. iPhone video) to record your role play, and upload to Blackboard for submission. Each person of the dyad completing the assignment should upload the video of themselves playing the role of interviewer.

Given the nature of the video assignment and the accompanying written assignment, we strongly urge students to begin meeting, doing practice runs, filming, and formulating feedback in their dyads as soon after their honours thesis is handed in, for best outcomes.
assessments.task_description:
This task will assess students' foundational interview skills and interpersonal communication skills. Students will submit a videotaped role-play of an interview scenario that is a minimum of 3 minutes to a maximum of 5 minutes in length.

The video role-play is recorded on ECHO360 or similar platform and uploaded to Blackboard by the due date.  

Please refer to your Learn.UQ course site for further information.   

The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: All students are required to complete this task and submit their video recorded role-play by the due date.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Written Peer Feedback and Personal Reflection on Video Role-play
assessments.due_date:
26/10/2026 5:00 pm

Given the nature of the video assignment and the accompanying written assignment, we strongly urge students to begin meeting, doing practice runs, filming, and formulating feedback in their dyads as soon after their honours thesis is handed in as possible, for best outcomes.
assessments.task_description:
Students will demonstrate their skills in critiquing interviewing and communication skills by giving written feedback on their partner's performance as the interviewer, singling out one evidence-based piece of advice on improvement for their partner with respect to either interviewing or communication skills (500 words of text maximum, with minimum 1 and maximum 2 references), and a similar piece of written feedback on their own performance as the interviewer, singling out one evidence-based piece of advice on improvement for themselves with respect to either interviewing or communication skills (500 words of text maximum, with minimum 1 and maximum 2 references). Note references here do not count against word count.

The written reflection will be uploaded to Blackboard by the due date.  

Please refer to your Learn.UQ course site for further information.  

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit via Blackboard by the due date.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Lecture: Scientist Practitioner in Action: Language in Health: Why words matter – Early pregnancy loss and psychological assessment as case studies

Guest lecturer Dr Marjolein Kammers will explore language as a clinical tool in healthcare, using examples from neuropsychological assessment and early pregnancy loss research, including her M-HELP intervention program and training.

Trigger Warning / Content Notice: This session contains discussion of pregnancy loss, trauma, grief, neurodevelopmental diagnoses, and related psychological experiences. Students are encouraged to consider their own wellbeing when engaging with the material and to access appropriate support if needed
learning_activities.topic_title: Lecture: Scientist Practitioner in Action: Language in Health: Why words matter – Early pregnancy loss and psychological assessment as case studies

Trigger Warning / Content Notice: This session contains discussion of pregnancy loss, trauma, grief, neurodevelopmental diagnoses, and related psychological experiences. Students are encouraged to consider their own wellbeing when engaging with the material and to access appropriate support if needed

learning_activities.topic:
Lecture: Scientist Practitioner in Action: Geropsychology in Clinical, Neuropsychology and Life Transition Contexts

Prof Nancy Pachana explores how the scientist practitioner role of the psychologist can help older adults live their best lives, in terms of cognitive, emotional and social wellbeing, across clinical, organisational, community and research contexts.
